era_context: The Late Bronze Age, a period of interconnected palace-based civilisations across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. The New Kingdom of Egypt under the 18th Dynasty (Thutmose III's successors) was at its height; the Hittite Empire dominated Anatolia; Mycenaean Greece was in the Late Helladic II period; the Shang Dynasty ruled China; and the Indus Valley Civilisation had already collapsed, with the Vedic period emerging in South Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (Maya civilisation emerging; limited contemporary written records)

events.title: Reign of Amenhotep III begins (approximate)
events.description: Amenhotep III ascended to the Egyptian throne, initiating one of the most prosperous and culturally productive reigns of the 18th Dynasty. His rule lasted approximately 38 years and was marked by extensive building programmes, diplomatic marriages with foreign powers (particularly the Mitanni), and the consolidation of Egyptian imperial power. The reign saw the construction of the Colossi of Memnon and the Temple of Luxor, and established Egypt as the dominant regional power in the Eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Regnal dates for Amenhotep III are established within ±2–3 years by Egyptological consensus, though absolute chronology for the 18th Dynasty remains debated. The accession date of c. 1390 BCE (conventional) places this event at approximately -1520 in the proleptic Gregorian calendar, but some scholars place it 10–15 years earlier.

events.title: Hittite consolidation under Šuppiluliuma I (early reign)
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I, one of the greatest Hittite kings, was consolidating power and expanding Hittite influence in Anatolia and northern Syria. His reign (c. 1344–1322 BCE in conventional chronology, or c. 1520–1498 in this proleptic system) saw the transformation of the Hittite state from a regional power into an imperial superpower. He conducted military campaigns against the Mitanni and established Hittite hegemony over much of the Levant.

sources.name: Hittite royal annals (clay tablets from Boğazköy)

events.certainty_note: Hittite chronology is debated; conventional dating places Šuppiluliuma I's reign c. 1344–1322 BCE, but some scholars argue for dates 50–100 years earlier or later. The assignment to -1520 is approximate within ±10 years.

events.title: Shang Dynasty consolidation in China
events.description: The Shang Dynasty, China's first historically attested dynasty, was in its mature phase. Oracle bone inscriptions from this period document a sophisticated state with a developed writing system, ritual practices, and military organisation. The Shang controlled the Yellow River valley and engaged in bronze-working, agriculture, and long-distance trade. This period saw the reign of kings such as Wu Ding or his successors, whose names appear in oracle bone records.
key_figures: Shang Dynasty rulers (Wu Ding or successors, names uncertain for this specific year)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Shang chronology is highly debated. Traditional Chinese dating places the Shang dynasty c. 1600–1046 BCE, but some scholars argue for dates 100–200 years later. Oracle bone inscriptions provide contemporary evidence of Shang existence and practices, but precise year-by-year dating is not possible. Assignment to -1520 is conventional.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional dating of Amenhotep III's accession to c. 1390 BCE is disputed by some scholars who argue for a 'low chronology' placing it c. 1380 BCE or even later. This would shift the assignment of this year by 10–20 years. Additionally, the Hittite chronology used here (placing Šuppiluliuma I in this period) is contested; some scholars place him 50–100 years earlier. Shang chronology remains one of the most contentious issues in East Asian history, with competing 'high,' 'middle,' and 'low' chronologies differing by over a century.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1520 BCE is heavily skewed toward the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East, where written records (Egyptian, Hittite, and cuneiform sources) provide relatively rich evidence. China is represented through oracle bone inscriptions, which are contemporary but limited in scope. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, Mesoamerica, and the Pacific have no written records for this year and are effectively invisible in the historical record. The surviving sources are predominantly from elite, court-sponsored contexts (royal inscriptions, temple records), which bias the record toward political and military events and away from economic, social, or demographic changes. Absolute chronology for this period remains uncertain by ±10–50 years depending on the region and scholarly school.

graph_edges.note: Amenhotep III's reign established the conditions for the religious and political upheaval under his son Amenhotep IV (Akhenaten)
Šuppiluliuma I's early consolidation led to his major military campaigns against the Mitanni in the following decades
